file_name: GSITECHNOLOGYINC_11_16_2009-EX-10.2-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT between SONY ELECTRONICS INC. and GSI TECHNOLOGY, INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: 1.4 Licensed Patent(s) The term "Licensed Patent" or "Licensed Patents" means any patent or patents issued at any time in any country (including any inventor's certificates, extension, reissued, renewal and reexamined patents), which (1) is based on any invention made on or prior to the Effective Date, including any patents for inventions disclosed in patent applications filed on or prior to the Effective Date, and issuing from any of those patent applications or any patent applications which are continuations, continuing applications, continuations-in-part or divisions of those patent applications, or on any foreign counterpart of any of those patent applications, (2) (a) is owned by SONY or any of its Affiliates or (b) under which SONY or its Affiliates have the right to grant licenses without payment by SONY or any of its Affiliates of additional royalties or other consideration to a third party (excluding payments for inventions made by that third party while employed by SONY or any of its Affiliates), and (3) would, in the absence of a license granted herein, be infringed by making, using, selling, offering for sale, importing or supplying any Competing Products (including, without limitation, the SRAM Products). "Licensed Patents" shall exclude the Transferred Patents.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 Licensed Patent(s) The term "Licensed Patent" or "Licensed Patents" means any patent or patents, in any country, which (1) is based on any invention at any time before the end of the term of this agreement, and (2) is owned by SONY or any entity. "Licensed Patents" shall exclude the Transferred Patents.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition broadens the scope of "Licensed Patents" beyond the effective date and beyond Sony and its affiliates. This introduces ambiguity and conflict regarding the patents covered by the license, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 154 - Contents and term of patent; provisional rights
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts patent law (35 U.S.C. § 154) which defines the term and duration of a patent. By removing key limitations on patent scope (date, affiliation), the contract language undermines the legal framework defining patent rights and the scope of licensed patents.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 154 - Contents and term of patent; provisional rights | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 154 - Contents and term of patent; provisional rights U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— (1) Contents.— Every patent shall contain a short title of the invention and a grant to the patentee, his heirs or assigns, of the right to exclude others from making, using, offering for sale, or selling the invention throughout the United States or importing the invention into the United States, and, if the invention is a process, of the right to exclude others from using, offering for sale or selling throughout the United States, or importing into the United States, products made by that process, referring to the specification for the particulars thereof. (2) Term.— Subject to the payment of fees under this title, such grant shall be for a term beginning on the date on which the patent issues and ending 20 years from the date on which the application for the patent was filed in the United States or, if the application contains a specific reference to an earlier filed application or applications under section 120, 121, 365(c), or 386(c), from the date on which the earliest such application was filed. (3) Priority.— Priority under section 119 , 365(a) , 365(b) , 386(a) , or 386(b) shall not be taken into account in determining the term of a patent. (4) Specification and drawing.— A copy of the specification and drawing shall be annexed to the patent and be a part of such patent. (b) Adjustment of Patent Term.— (1) Patent term guarantees.— (A) Guarantee of prompt patent and trademark office responses.— Subject to the limitations under paragraph (2), if the issue of an original patent is delayed due to the failure of the Patent and Trademark Office to— (i) provide at least one of the notifications under section 132 or a notice of allowance under section 151 n
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract language broadens the scope of "Licensed Patents" by removing the restriction based on the effective date and the affiliation with SONY or its affiliates. This contradicts the general principles of patent law, particularly regarding the defined term and ownership of patents, undermining the legal framework that defines patent rights, as described in the Law Explanation. The modified language introduces ambiguity regarding the specific patents covered, potentially leading to disputes and rendering the contract unenforceable, which is a feature of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction. While the provided law snippets do not explicitly define the scope of licensed patents with respect to effective dates or company affiliations, they do emphasize the patentee's right to exclude others (35 U.S.C. § 154), a right tied to a specific invention and patent term. By broadening the definition of "Licensed Patents," the modification could create uncertainty regarding which patents are subject to the license and for how long, diminishing the patentee's defined rights.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Grant by SONY SONY, on behalf of itself and its Affiliates, hereby grants to PURCHASER a worldwide, non-exclusive, fully paid-up, royalty-free license (a) under the Licensed Patents to make, have made, use, offer to sell, sell, otherwise dispose of, and import any Competing Products (including, without limitation, the SRAM Products); and (b) to use, reproduce, modify, prepare derivative works of, perform, display, and otherwise practice and exploit in any manner any and all of the SRAM Intellectual Property in connection with the use and exploitation of the 3 Transferred Assets, and to make, have made, use, offer to sell, sell, otherwise dispose of, and import Competing Products (including, without limitation, SRAM Products).
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Grant by SONY SONY, on behalf of itself only, hereby grants to PURCHASER a license (a) under the Licensed Patents to make, have made, use, offer to sell, sell, otherwise dispose of, and import any Competing Products (including, without limitation, the SRAM Products); and (b) to use, reproduce, modify, prepare derivative works of, perform, display, and otherwise practice and exploit in any manner any and all of the SRAM Intellectual Property in connection with the use and exploitation of the 3 Transferred Assets, and to make, have made, use, offer to sell, sell, otherwise dispose of, and import Competing Products (including, without limitation, SRAM Products).
perturbation.explanation: The change removes "its Affiliates" and "fully paid-up, royalty-free" from the grant of license. This modification introduces ambiguity, as it is unclear if the license extends to Sony's affiliates, and whether royalties may be demanded. This ambiguity can be used to attempt to collect royalties.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification creates a potential conflict with 35 U.S.C. § 262, which addresses the rights of joint owners of a patent. If SONY's affiliates are joint owners of the licensed patents but are not explicitly included in the license grant, it could lead to infringement claims against PURCHASER despite the agreement's intent to provide a license. It is also a false statement as SONY cannot provide the stated license if it has any affiliates involved in owning the same patent.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes 'its Affiliates' from the license grant, which creates a potential contradiction with 35 U.S.C. § 262 if the affiliates are joint owners of the patent. The law snippets state, 'In the absence of any agreement to the contrary, each of the joint owners of a patent may make, use, offer to sell, or sell the patented invention... without the consent of and without accounting to the other owners.' By excluding the affiliates from the license, SONY might not have the authority to grant the license on their behalf, thus contradicting the law that allows joint owners to operate independently. The removal of 'fully paid-up, royalty-free' also introduces the potential for demanding royalties, contradicting the original intent of a fully paid license and impacting the licensee's rights under the patent.

perturbation.original_text: 9.9 Applicable Law This IP Agreement and the legal relations among the Parties will be governed by and construed in accordance with the rules and substantive Laws of the State of California, United States of America, without regard to conflicts of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.9 Applicable Law This IP Agreement and the legal relations among the Parties will be governed by and construed in accordance with the rules and substantive Laws of the State of Nevada, United States of America, with regard to conflicts of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies California law, but the modified version changes it to Nevada law and includes conflicts of law provisions. This could lead to uncertainty if key aspects of the contract rely on interpretations specific to California law, such as those affecting IP rights, introducing conflict in the application of governing law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Switching the applicable law from California to Nevada and adding "with regard to conflicts of law provisions" creates a legal contradiction with the presumed original intent. It could contradict California Commercial Code § 1105, especially if the agreement was originally drafted with specific reliance on California's interpretation of commercial or contract law principles. The change now demands Nevada law and requires a determination about which state's laws apply via conflict of laws rules, potentially undermining predictability and enforcement.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 1. Short Title, Construction, Application and Subject Matter of the Code [1101 - 1108] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1105. If any provision or clause of this code or its application to any person or circumstance is held invalid, the invalidity does not affect other provisions or applications of the code which can be given effect without the invalid provision or application, and to this end the provisions of this code are severable. (Added by renumbering Section 1108 by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 14. Effective January 1, 2007.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from California to Nevada and introduces conflicts of law provisions. This contradicts the original intent, potentially undermining predictability and enforcement, as noted in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that this could lead to uncertainty if key aspects of the contract rely on California-specific interpretations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses severability within the California Commercial Code, it does not address the core issue of changing the governing law itself, which could invalidate or significantly alter the application of the original contract terms. Therefore, the modification introduces a contradiction by disrupting the initially chosen legal framework.
